case_text: Net NPA of just 0.42% — asset quality is exceptionally clean by Indian banking standards.
Capital cushion (19.9% CAR) sits well above the 11.9% regulatory floor, room to grow without raising fresh capital.
Advances (+11.9% YoY) and deposits (+11.6% YoY) are growing in lockstep — balanced, not over-extended growth.
CASA ratio of 33.6% is moderate, not best-in-class — more reliance on costlier term deposits than some peers.
The HDFC Ltd merger (July 2023) means pre-FY24 multi-year comparisons aren't meaningful — read older trend data with care.
Sector-wide NIM compression as deposit costs catch up with loan repricing is worth watching over the next few quarters.
NIM of 4.30% is the highest among India's large banks — directly boosts core lending profitability.
Net NPA of just 0.37%, with a 75.4% provisioning coverage ratio — an unusually strong cushion against future shocks.
Loan growth (11.5% YoY) is broad-based across retail, SME and business banking, not reliant on one segment.
A one-time RBI-mandated provision (₹1,283 crore on agri-sector loans) dented Q3 profit growth — regulatory directives can bite even strong banks.
CASA ratio of 39% has been gradually moderating as depositors chase higher-yielding term deposits, a sector-wide funding-cost headwind.
Contingent liabilities are very large in absolute terms — typical for a bank this size, but worth understanding before treating it as a red flag on its own.
Net NPA (0.39%) and Gross NPA (1.57%) are both at their lowest in over two decades — a structural improvement, not a one-off quarter.

CASA ratio held nearly flat at 39.13% even as the sector saw outflows to term deposits — its 23,000+ branch network and government/PSU banking relationships are a funding edge competitors can't easily copy.
Loan book grew 15.14% YoY, ahead of its own guidance, prompting management to raise FY26 credit-growth guidance to 13–15%.
Capital adequacy (14.04%) is comfortably above the regulatory minimum but is the thinnest cushion of the three banks on this dashboard — less room to absorb a shock without raising capital.
NIM has been on a gradual multi-quarter downtrend as deposit costs catch up; management is guiding to hold it 'above 3%,' not expand it.
As a majority government-owned bank, financial-inclusion mandates and policy priorities can occasionally take precedence over pure profit-maximisation — a structural, not one-off, consideration.
Capital adequacy of 22.6% is the strongest cushion of any bank on this dashboard — more than double the regulatory minimum.
Net NPA (0.31%) and Gross NPA (1.30%) both improved YoY — a genuinely strengthening asset-quality trend, not just a stable one.
CASA ratio of 41.3% is the highest of the five banks tracked here — a structural low-cost-funding edge.
NIM compressed sharply to 4.54% from 4.93% a year ago, and management itself flagged only a 'moderate' recovery ahead, not a sharp one.
Provisions jumped on stress in credit cards, microfinance, and retail commercial vehicles — a segment to keep watching, not a closed issue.
The stock has historically traded at a premium valuation versus peers, leaving less room for error if growth or margins disappoint further.
Gross NPA (1.40%) and Net NPA (0.42%) both improved sequentially and YoY — asset quality genuinely getting better.
Net profit rebounded sharply (+27.5% QoQ) on a 36.7% drop in provisions — the earlier credit-cost spike looks like it's normalising.
CASA ratio of 39% remains, in the bank's own words, 'among the best across large peer banks' despite the sector-wide funding-cost squeeze.
NIM compressed to 3.64% from 3.73% the prior quarter, with no clear sign yet of a quick reversal.
Provision coverage ratio fell sharply to 70% from 78% a year ago, even as headline NPA ratios improved — a thinner safety cushion against any fresh stress.
CASA has slid for several straight quarters (41% a year ago to 39% now) — sector-wide, but worth tracking closely for Axis specifically.
Asset quality keeps improving, not just holding steady: Gross NPA 1.21% and Net NPA 0.47%, both down from the prior quarter.
AUM grew 22% YoY, on track for management's own ~22-23% guidance and crossing ₹5 lakh crore in scale shortly after this quarter.
Capital adequacy of 21.45% is far above the regulatory minimum, with cost of borrowing easing slightly (7.45%, down 7bps QoQ) even as rates stayed elevated sector-wide.
Reported consolidated profit actually fell ~6% YoY this quarter due to an accelerated provisioning charge — core profit (excluding that one-off) still grew 23%, but headline optics were weak.

Unlike every bank on this dashboard, Bajaj Finance doesn't headline a single NIM% figure — a reminder that NBFC disclosures aren't a drop-in replacement for bank disclosures, even when the underlying business is healthy.
MSME growth has been deliberately muted for several quarters (management itself guides 2-3 more soft quarters there) — a self-imposed brake, not a crisis, but a real near-term growth drag.
AUM grew ~21% YoY with broad-based strength — SME loans up 41%, home loans up 23%, and its largest segment (vehicle finance) regaining momentum after a soft patch.
Capital adequacy of ~19.8% is comfortably above the 15% regulatory minimum, real headroom to sustain 20%+ AUM growth ambitions.
Profitability stayed strong (ROE around 18-19%) even while investing in newer, faster-growing lines like gold loans and SME finance.

Asset quality is the recurring worry: Gross NPA under RBI norms (4.63%) reads meaningfully worse than the Ind-AS 'Stage 3' figure (~3.4%) the company also reports — know which number you're looking at before comparing Chola's asset quality to a bank's GNPA%, since they're not computed the same way.
Vehicle finance, still its single largest segment, has seen rising delinquencies tied to weaker rural economies more than once in the past year — a cyclical risk, not a one-off.
Trades at a premium valuation (P/E well above peer median) — several analysts have flagged this as pricing in close-to-flawless execution, leaving little room for a misstep.
NIM actually expanded to 8.58%, up from 8.48% a year ago — a rare case of margin growth while most lenders on this dashboard are seeing compression.

Asset quality keeps improving (Gross Stage 3 4.54%, Net Stage 3 2.38%, both down YoY and sequentially) despite serving a structurally riskier customer base — used commercial vehicles, MSME, rural borrowers — than almost any other lender here.
A pending ₹39,618 crore equity infusion from MUFG Bank (Japan's largest bank) would materially strengthen the capital base and is a strong external vote of confidence in the franchise.

Reported profit fell 22% YoY this quarter — almost entirely because last year's quarter included a one-off gain from selling its housing-finance subsidiary; core profit (excluding that) actually grew over 21%, but the headline number alone looks weak.
Cost of liabilities (8.69%) is the highest of any NBFC on this dashboard — a structurally more expensive funding base that comes with serving a riskier, more rural/retail borrower profile.
Heavily tied to India's commercial-vehicle cycle and rural/semi-urban incomes — a freight slowdown or weak monsoon flows through to this book faster than it would for a diversified bank.
Gross NPA fell to 2.04% — the lowest in the bank's history — backed by a 92.73% provision coverage ratio.
Loan growth accelerated to 14.65% YoY, with RAM (Retail/Agri/MSME) now 61.8% of advances — a real shift toward higher-quality, less concentrated lending.
Five-year net profit CAGR above 50% reflects a genuine post-recapitalisation turnaround, not a one-quarter blip.
NIM kept compressing to 2.79% from 2.96% the prior quarter — the lowest of any bank on this dashboard, with management itself flagging further compression below 2.75% as a real risk.
Capital adequacy slid from 16.54% to 15.29% in a single quarter, mostly from risk-weighted-asset growth — still well above the regulatory floor, but the trend is worth tracking.
CASA ratio (38.45%) is the lowest of any bank here and continuing to slip — a structurally thinner cheap-funding cushion than the larger private banks.
Net NPA fell to just 0.32% (from 0.41% a year ago), with provision coverage near 97% — among the strongest asset-quality positions of any PSU bank.
Capital adequacy improved meaningfully to 16.77%, up 136 bps from a year ago — a genuinely strengthening position, not just a stable one.
Retail loan growth was sharp (+18.9% YoY), led by vehicle loans (+35.7%) and housing (+14.5%) — a real shift toward higher-quality retail lending.

NIM fell to 2.52% (global) from 2.93% a year ago — among the steepest margin compression of any bank on this dashboard, with management guiding for further near-term pressure before stabilising around FY27.
CASA ratio (37.1%) is among the lowest here, and continuing to slip — a structurally costlier funding base than top private banks.
This quarter's profit included a ₹700 crore one-off treasury gain from a stake sale — worth separating core banking profit from one-time gains when judging earnings quality.
Net NPA improved to 0.42% — the bank's best in a decade — with Gross NPA down to 1.72%, both moving the right direction for several straight quarters.
CASA ratio crossed 32% with record fee income (+19% YoY), genuine revenue diversification rather than just margin-chasing.

A landmark Blackstone capital infusion is expected to lift capital adequacy by roughly 50bps in Q4 FY26 alone, with more capital still arriving through FY28 — a multi-year capital tailwind most banks here don't have lined up.

Loan growth (under 11% YoY) is the slowest of any bank on this dashboard — management is deliberately prioritising 'quality of growth' over pace, which is defensible, but it does mean ceding scale gains to faster-growing peers right now.

Return on equity (12.9% over the last 3 years per Screener) trails most peers here — a structurally lower profitability ceiling unless the Blackstone capital and mix-shift translate into materially higher returns.

Contingent liabilities are large in absolute terms (₹2.17 lakh crore) — standard for a bank this size, but worth understanding what's inside that figure rather than treating it as a red flag on its own.

NIM (5.76%) and CASA (51.64%) are both the highest of any bank on this dashboard by a wide margin — a genuinely differentiated funding and margin profile for a bank still under 8 years old in its current form.
Loan book grew 20.93% YoY, the fastest here, with 89% of that growth spread across mortgages, vehicle loans, consumer loans, business banking and wholesale — a diversified mix, not one risky segment.
Asset quality kept improving even through rapid growth: Gross NPA fell to 1.69%, with MFI-related slippages specifically called out as improving.
NIM is reported 'gross of IBPC & sell-down' — a different methodology than most banks use, so this figure isn't perfectly apples-to-apples with other banks' NIM on this dashboard.
Net NPA ticked up slightly (0.53% from 0.52%) even as Gross NPA improved — a small but real divergence worth watching given past stress in its MFI/unsecured book.

Return on assets, by management's own admission on the earnings call, remains low (around 0.5%) — a bank still proving out the profitability of its post-merger retail transformation, not yet a finished story.
Gross NPA fell sharply to 3.33% from 5.02% just one quarter earlier — a remarkable 169bps improvement suggesting the worst of its microfinance stress cycle may be behind it.
Profit rebounded 84% sequentially on materially better collections, with microfinance (EEB) collection efficiency holding strong at 98.2%.
Capital adequacy of 17.33% remains comfortably above regulatory requirements, leaving room to keep growing without needing fresh capital soon.

That headline 5.9% NIM is the highest on this dashboard, but it reflects high-yield, high-risk microfinance lending, not efficient banking — it comes paired with the lowest CASA ratio here (27.3%) and one of the higher Gross NPA ratios (3.33%). Read it as 'compensation for risk taken,' not 'best margins.'
CASA ratio kept sliding — 31.7% a year ago to 28.0% to 27.3% now — the lowest and fastest-eroding cheap-deposit base of any bank tracked here.

Profit is still down 51.7% YoY despite the sequential rebound, and the bank sold ₹6,872 crore of bad microfinance loans to asset reconstruction companies in December 2025 — a sign the cleanup is real, but also that there was a lot to clean up.
Net profit jumped 26% YoY with provisions down 34% YoY even as the loan book grew a healthy 19.3% — growth and credit discipline improving together, not at each other's expense.

Received in-principle RBI approval to transition into a Universal Bank — a structural unlock that should lower funding costs over time and let it compete more directly with full-service private banks.
Asset quality has been stable for three straight quarters (GNPA 2.30%, NNPA 0.88% unchanged), weathering the small-finance-bank sector's recent microfinance stress better than several peers.
NIM kept compressing — 5.7%, down from 5.9% a year ago — as competition for deposits stays intense; management itself called the deposit environment 'highly competitive.'
CASA ratio slipped from 31.0% a year ago to 29.0% now — the same cheap-deposit erosion playing out across nearly every bank on this dashboard, not unique to AU SFB.
At least one tracker has flagged the valuation as 'very expensive' with a PEG ratio above 2 — a reminder that strong execution doesn't automatically mean a cheap stock.

Gross NPA hit a record low of 2.08% (from 3.34% a year ago) with Net NPA at just 0.45% and provision coverage above 94% — asset quality now genuinely rivals the better private banks, not just other PSU peers.
Profit grew 25.6% YoY for the seventh straight quarter of 18%+ YoY growth — a sustained trend, not a one-off beat.
Raised ₹3,500 crore via AT1 bonds during the quarter, reinforcing an already-comfortable 16.50% capital adequacy ratio with room to keep growing.

A meaningful share of this quarter's profit growth came from a one-time ~₹1,800 crore gain on reclassifying two subsidiaries (Canara Robeco AMC, Canara HSBC Life) as associates — worth separating from core lending performance when judging earnings quality.

NIM kept falling to 2.50% from 2.80% across FY25, and CASA (29.52%) is among the lower end of this dashboard, with management's own near-term target being just 32% — still below its level two years ago.

CET1 — the purest measure of core capital — at 12.37% is lower than most PSU peers with comparable asset quality, per India Ratings; the comfortable headline capital ratio leans more on AT1/Tier-2 instruments than some peers.

Volume growth (4% UVG) is the highest in 12 quarters, and management described demand trends as showing 'early signs of recovery' on the Q3 earnings call — a genuine turning point after several quarters of soft volumes, not just narrative.
ROCE of 31.61% and zero long-term debt — this company creates substantial returns on capital without leverage; that's a quality of earnings test most companies fail.

The portfolio rotation is real: ice cream (low-margin) is now demerged and separately listed; premium digital-native brands Minimalist and OZiva are fully owned; quick-commerce now ~3% of sales and doubling every quarter.

The reported 121% profit surge is almost entirely a one-time accounting gain (₹4,485 crore exceptional item from the ice cream demerger) — adjusted PAT from continuing operations grew about 1% YoY, which is the real underlying number.

EBITDA margins compressed 70 bps YoY to 23.3% — palm oil and crude-linked raw material costs are the named culprit; management guided to staying within 22–23%, not expanding back to historical 24–25%.

Revenue growth of 5.7% (and 5-year sales CAGR of just ~6.5%) is unspectacular for a company trading at over 49x FY27E earnings per analyst estimates — the valuation assumes flawless execution on premiumisation and quick-commerce scaling, with little room for a miss.

ROCE of 44.26% is the highest of any company on this dashboard — a direct result of the cigarette business's structurally high margins and low capital requirements. The business model itself generates extraordinary returns on the capital it actually deploys.

FMCG-Others (non-cigarette packaged foods, personal care, agarbattis) grew +11.1% revenue YoY with EBITDA margins expanding 145 bps — the diversification away from cigarettes is actually delivering results, not just a stated ambition.
Dividend yield of ~2% is the highest among FMCG peers tracked here, backed by consistent FCF from the cigarette franchise. Management declared ₹6.50/share interim dividend this quarter.

A cigarette excise duty hike effective February 1, 2026 (₹2.05–₹8.50 per stick by length, replacing compensation cess) came just after Q3 ended — so this quarter's results don't reflect it yet. The stock fell ~20% from its 52-week high near ₹471 to ₹303–318 around results, pricing in the regulatory risk the numbers haven't yet shown.
Cigarette EBIT margins already contracted 140 bps YoY to 56.7% even before the excise hike, on rising leaf tobacco costs — margin pressure was already building in the core business.

~70–75% of total profit still comes from cigarettes: a business facing annual regulatory risk, secular volume headwinds from health awareness, and an illicit trade market that grows every time legal prices are raised. The entire conglomerate's financial health is structurally dependent on one regulated, shrinking-by-design category.

AI is becoming a real, measurable revenue line, not just a talking point: annualised AI services revenue hit $1.8 billion, growing 17.3% sequentially — management's stated ambition is to become 'the world's largest AI-led technology services company.'

Deal TCV of $9.3 billion this quarter (with a North America mega deal) gives strong revenue visibility — a forward indicator that current bookings exceed current billing, a healthy sign for future quarters.
Operating margin held steady at 25.2% despite wage hikes — disciplined cost management even while growth stayed modest.

Constant-currency revenue actually declined 2.6% YoY — the headline 4.9% INR growth is mostly currency translation, not real organic demand. This is the structural GenAI-era story the project plan flagged: pricing and volume pressure in traditional delivery work, even at the sector's largest, most efficient player.

Reported net profit fell YoY, driven by exceptional items this quarter — management says this doesn't reflect core business weakness, but it's worth separating headline profit from underlying trend regardless.

AI revenue, while growing fast, is still a small fraction of TCS's ~$30bn annual revenue base — the disclosed dollar figure ($1.8bn annualised) needs context against total scale, not just its growth rate, before treating it as a turnaround signal.

Attrition fell to 12.3% LTM, down from 14.3% the prior quarter — a real, improving trend, and the lowest of the two IT companies tracked here, suggesting better employee retention even amid sector-wide AI-driven uncertainty.
Management raised FY26 revenue guidance (to 3.0-3.5% constant currency) rather than cutting it — a vote of confidence from the company closest to its own numbers, not just analyst optimism.
Deal TCV of $4.8bn with 57% net new business signals real incremental demand, not just contract renewals — a healthier mix than pure renewal-driven bookings.

Constant-currency revenue growth of just 1.7% YoY is barely positive — the same structural pattern as TCS, the clearest evidence yet that this is a sector-wide GenAI-era growth slowdown, not one company's execution problem.

Reported operating margin (18.4%) was meaningfully below the adjusted figure (21.2%) due to a one-time Labour Codes charge — worth using the adjusted number for trend analysis, but the gap itself shows how much one-off items can swing a single quarter's headline margin.

Unlike TCS, Infosys doesn't publish a standalone AI-revenue growth metric — making it genuinely harder to judge whether its AI investments (branded 'Topaz') are translating into revenue at a comparable pace, or whether the absence of a disclosed number is itself information.

Revenue grew 15.1% YoY while EBITDA margin simultaneously expanded 260bps to 31.9% — growth and margin expansion together is the harder combination to pull off, and most pharma peers this quarter are managing only one or the other.

Global Innovative Medicines (the higher-margin, less price-competitive segment) grew 13.2% ex-milestone and now makes up 21.2% of sales — a real, working strategic shift away from commodity generics pricing pressure, not just a stated ambition.
Maintains India's #1 pharma position with market share still increasing (8.4%), plus a fortress balance sheet ($3.2 billion net cash) to keep funding the pipeline without urgency.

US business was flat (+0.6%) — Innovative Medicines growth was offset by generic price erosion, the same structural US pricing pressure hitting the whole Indian pharma export sector. Better-hedged here, not immune.

An exceptional charge of ₹489.5 crore this quarter (wage-code gratuity + a legal settlement provision) means the headline 16% profit growth somewhat overstates underlying performance — adjusted net profit grew a more modest 9.9%.
116 ANDAs remain pending FDA approval — normal pipeline depth, but every pending filing carries unpredictable regulatory-timeline risk that can delay expected launches by quarters, sometimes years.

India business grew a strong 19% YoY (organic growth over 17%), led by innovation brands, new launches, and the acquired Stugeron portfolio — proof the domestic franchise is healthy even as other markets wobble.

Actively pivoting toward biosimilars (trastuzumab, adalimumab in pipeline) and complex generics — a deliberate strategic shift away from the commodity small-molecule pricing pressure visible in this quarter's results.
Net cash surplus of ₹3,069 crore and positive free cash flow (₹374 crore) even in a tougher quarter — balance sheet strength to fund the pipeline pivot without urgency.

North America revenue fell 16% YoY, driven specifically by declining Lenalidomide sales as the volume-capped settlement period (from the 2020 Celgene/BMS patent settlement) approaches its January 31, 2026 full-volume-unlock date — a single-molecule concentration risk now visibly playing out in the numbers, not a hypothetical risk anymore.

EBITDA margin compressed sharply to 23.5% from 27.5% a year ago — even the adjusted figure (24.8%, excluding a one-time labour-code provision) is still down nearly 300bps YoY, a real deterioration, not just a one-off accounting item.
Net profit fell 14% YoY and 16% sequentially — alongside revenue growth slowing to 4.4% and margin compression, three separate data points pointing the same direction this quarter.
Highest-ever quarterly volumes (667,769 units) and a record December, with dealer inventory lean at just 3 days — real demand, not a stocking illusion.
Exports are now roughly 45% of India's total PV exports, about double nearest rival Hyundai's share — a genuine structural export moat.
Zero long-term debt and a large net-cash position fund its ₹70,000 crore FY31 investment plan (8 new SUVs, EV/hybrid rollout) without straining the balance sheet.
PV market share has slipped from around 50% in 2020 to below 40% now, as SUV-focused rivals (Mahindra, Tata, Hyundai) grow faster than the overall market.
EBITDA margin compressed 210 bps YoY on commodity costs, FX and a one-time labour-law provision — the GST-driven volume surge came at a real profitability cost.
Its first EV launched only in 2025, well behind Tata Motors and Mahindra, who already hold established EV market share — a late mover in the segment reshaping the industry.

India domestic PV+EV business hit its highest-ever quarterly wholesale (171,000 units, +22% YoY) and its first-ever 200,000+ retail month — the core India franchise is healthy independent of JLR's troubles.
The Oct-Nov 2025 demerger of the commercial-vehicle business unlocks a cleaner, focused PV+JLR entity — CV cyclicality and capital needs no longer distort this stock's numbers.
JLR's cyber incident is a one-off operational shock, not a structural demand problem — production returned to normal by mid-November and management guides to a significant Q4 recovery.

A cyberattack on JLR in late 2025 halted production for weeks, wiping out roughly 39% of JLR's quarterly revenue and pushing the whole consolidated entity to a net loss — a stark reminder of how much this stock still depends on a single UK-based luxury subsidiary.

JLR separately faces US tariffs, a China luxury-tax headwind, and the planned wind-down of legacy Jaguar models ahead of an unproven all-new electric Jaguar lineup — multiple overlapping risks stacked on top of the cyber recovery.
FY26 JLR guidance was already cut to a 0-2% EBIT margin with a £2.2-2.5bn free cash outflow even before fully absorbing the cyber-incident hit.
#1 revenue market share in SUVs (24.1%) and #1 in tractors (44.0%) — genuine category leadership in both core manufacturing businesses at once.

Financial services arm (Mahindra Finance) grew PAT 97% YoY while keeping asset quality strong, and two 'Growth Gems' — Mahindra Logistics and Mahindra Lifespaces — turned sharply profitable this quarter, showing diversification is starting to pay off.
Sold 41,000+ electric SUVs since its March 2025 EV launch, with management signalling readiness for a Last Mile Mobility (electric 3W/4W) IPO — real EV traction, not just plans.

US tractor exports were choked for eight to nine months by tariffs as high as 50% before a trade resolution brought them down to 18% — a reminder that even 'domestic' Indian manufacturers carry real global trade-policy exposure.

Realization/pricing growth was notably weaker than volume growth this quarter — growth is increasingly volume-led, not price-led, which caps margin expansion if commodity costs (copper, aluminium, precious metals) keep rising.
The group's complexity — auto, farm, financial services, IT services, logistics, real estate all under one ticker — makes it harder to value cleanly against pure-play auto peers.
Electric two- and three-wheelers now contribute 25% of domestic revenue, both individually crossing ₹1,000 crore in quarterly revenue — EV transition is already meaningful, not speculative.

Export volumes surpassed 600,000 units for the first time in 15 quarters, with KTM+Triumph domestic volumes/revenue up roughly 50% YoY — premiumization and geographic diversification happening simultaneously.
Record EBITDA margin (20.8%) even while absorbing lower-margin EV mix and cost inflation shows real pricing power and cost discipline.

Domestic motorcycle volume growth was a muted 3% YoY even with GST-rationalisation tailwinds and festive demand — most of this quarter's strength came from exports and premiumization, not core domestic demand.

Long-term debt has risen sharply (from roughly ₹759 crore to over ₹6,200 crore within a year) as the company funds its financing subsidiary and the KTM Austria transaction — a real shift from its historically debt-free balance sheet.
A key export market, Nigeria, continues to show negative YoY sales — a reminder that Bajaj's export-heavy model carries real emerging-market currency and demand volatility.
Royal Enfield's EBITDA margin (26.6% standalone) is best-in-class for the entire Indian two-wheeler industry — brand pricing power no domestic competitor currently matches.

Crossed 1 million motorcycles sold year-to-date, and approved a brownfield capacity expansion (14.6 lakh → 20 lakh units/year) — real, funded confidence in sustained demand, not just a one-quarter bump.
VECV posted market-share gains across every commercial-vehicle segment it competes in, adding a second growth engine uncorrelated with two-wheeler demand cycles.

Analysts flag that this quarter's domestic volume strength was largely a pull-forward effect from the September 2025 GST rate cut — demand has since 'stabilised,' meaning this growth rate may not repeat.
Management's explicit 'growth over profitability' stance means margin expansion is likely to stay capped even as volumes grow — a strategic choice investors should price in, not assume away.

Royal Enfield remains almost entirely a single-brand, single-category bet (90%+ of revenue) — any shift in premium-motorcycle demand or new EV-native competitors hits Eicher harder than more diversified auto peers.
Consolidated net income before exceptional items jumped 38.7% YoY to ₹7,245 crore — a clean, comparable profit number, not flattered by one-offs the way the prior year's headline PAT was.

Deleveraging is real and fast: net debt/EBITDA fell from 1.86x to 1.29x in a single year while still funding heavy 5G, fibre and data-centre capex (₹16,066 crore this quarter alone) — rare to cut debt and invest aggressively at the same time.
The Homes (broadband) business grew revenue 37.3% YoY with 1.1 million net customer adds this quarter — a genuine second growth engine beyond mobile, still early in its penetration curve.

Management itself said India's pricing architecture is 'fundamentally broken' by unlimited-data plans that cap revenue per user — ARPU actually dipped slightly quarter-on-quarter, and a real tariff-hike catalyst remains more hoped-for than confirmed.

Jio is pulling ahead in subscriber acquisition using fixed wireless access (FWA) for faster broadband rollout — the market-share gap between the two leaders is described as widening, not stable, in Airtel's own segment commentary.

Finance costs remain elevated relative to the pace of deleveraging, and international operations (Africa capex, INR depreciation) add a layer of currency and geopolitical cost sensitivity on top of the core India business.

Jio and Retail together contributed over 55% of consolidated EBITDA for the first time in FY26 — real, structural diversification away from the commodity energy cycle that used to define this stock, not just a talking point.

FY26 was a record year on every headline metric (revenue ₹11.76 lakh crore, EBITDA ₹2.08 lakh crore, PAT) despite a rough Q4 — the diversified portfolio genuinely absorbed a bad quarter in one segment (O2C) without dragging the whole company down.

Management describes the Jio Platforms IPO as 'fairly imminent' — a listing would crystallise Jio's standalone value (roughly ₹1.46 lakh crore FY26 revenue, ~₹7,900 crore quarterly PAT) separately from the conglomerate discount RIL currently trades at.

Q4 consolidated PAT fell 12.6% YoY and 9% QoQ to ₹16,971 crore, driven almost entirely by O2C margin compression from Middle East supply-chain disruption, higher crude premiums, and windfall taxes (SAED) — proof the energy business still swings group profit meaningfully, diversification narrative aside.

Retail's EBITDA margin compression (7.9%, down YoY) reflects the cost of competing in quick-commerce against well-funded, singularly-focused rivals like Blinkit and Zepto — an expensive land-grab with no confirmed payoff timeline yet.

One independent analysis flagged a weak trailing ROE (~9.5%) against a P/E premium to the broader market — a valuation that assumes the Jio/Retail growth story executes cleanly, leaving little room for O2C-style disappointments to repeat.

Consolidated PAT jumped 45.6% YoY to a record ₹41,424 crore for the full year, with subsidiaries HPCL, MRPL, OVL and OPaL delivering what the company itself called 'remarkable improvement' — diversification beyond pure upstream E&P is genuinely paying off.

New-well gas (priced higher than legacy nomination gas) now makes up 17% of production and over a fifth of nomination-gas revenue — a structural mix-shift toward better realizations as ONGC keeps investing in newer fields to offset natural decline in older ones.

A record FY26 dividend of ₹13.25/share (₹16,669 crore total, ~51% payout) makes ONGC one of India's most reliable large-cap dividend payers — a genuine, growing cash return to shareholders, not a one-off.

Strip out the subsidiaries and the core story is softer: full-year domestic E&P revenue actually declined 3.4% as weaker average crude prices through the year offset production gains — the headline profit jump leans heavily on refining/downstream units, not the upstream business ONGC is fundamentally valued on.

International operations (ONGC Videsh) revenue fell 22% YoY in Q4 — the overseas diversification arm, meant to reduce dependence on ageing domestic fields, underperformed even in a quarter when domestic subsidiaries did well.

The petrochemicals arm (OPaL) posted a full-year loss of ₹382 crore — not every leg of ONGC's downstream diversification strategy is working yet, a reminder that vertical integration doesn't guarantee profitability in every segment.
Q4 profit and EBITDA both grew double digits even as tonnes sold declined — real evidence of pricing and cost discipline (better realizations, lower stripping costs), not just a volume story.

A ₹5.25/share final dividend continues Coal India's reputation as one of India's most dependable large-cap income stocks — the government's fiscal reliance on CIL dividends provides real, if imperfect, downside protection for the payout.

Genuine, if early, diversification: listing of subsidiaries Bharat Coking Coal and CMPDI, a commissioned 100MW solar plant, and a new renewable-energy joint venture all point toward reducing pure-thermal-coal dependence over time.

The core mining business shrank in FY26: production down 1.7% and offtake down 2.4% for the full year, with closing inventory up 21% YoY — coal is being mined faster than it's being sold, a real demand-side or logistics problem, not a rounding error.

Full-year PAT fell nearly 12% and EBITDA fell 11% despite the strong headline Q4 — that quarterly beat leaned on a 30% jump in 'other income' and retrospective accounting adjustments per independent analysis, a weaker underlying operating trend than the Q4 print alone suggests.

India's fast-growing renewable and solar capacity is a structural, long-duration headwind to thermal coal demand growth — even as government policy pushes CIL to expand output and cut coal imports, nothing guarantees the demand side of that plan materialises on schedule.

India delivered its best-ever annual volumes (22.5 million tonnes, +8% YoY) at a standalone EBITDA margin around 24-25% — the core domestic business is firing on both volume and margin at once, a rare combination.

A structured, disclosed cost-transformation programme has delivered ₹10,868 crore in cumulative benefits and directly explains 320bps of the year's EBITDA margin improvement — evidence this isn't just a favourable pricing cycle, but real operational execution.

Management is guiding for a further 2 million tonnes of India volume growth in FY27 from the Kalinganagar ramp-up, plus continued margin expansion — a company describing forward momentum with specific, checkable numbers rather than vague optimism.

Europe remains a genuine drag: Tata Steel UK posted an EBITDA loss (₹591 crore) this quarter, and Tata Steel Netherlands has received multiple regulatory non-compliance notices plus over €20 million in penalties, with the local Environment Agency signalling intent to revoke permits and force an early closure of coke/gas plants — a real, live regulatory threat to a sizeable chunk of the business.

The FY26 profit surge is measured against an unusually weak FY25 base (full-year PAT +215% YoY) — a favourable comparison that flatters the growth rate more than it reflects a fundamentally transformed earnings level in absolute terms.

Global steel markets face persistent Chinese overcapacity and dumping pressure — India's domestic infrastructure-driven demand has insulated Tata Steel so far, but the company remains exposed to global pricing cycles it doesn't control.

Record quarterly revenue and an EBITDA beat (₹8,634 crore, +35% YoY) came even with a major blast furnace offline for upgrades — the underlying operating business is genuinely strong, independent of the one-off BPSL accounting gain.

The BPSL joint-venture deal with JFE Steel cut net debt by roughly ₹26,000-37,000 crore in a single quarter — a real, structural balance-sheet improvement, not just an accounting event, freeing capacity for the company's large approved capex programme.

A disclosed, board-approved capacity roadmap (31.9 to 62 million tonnes per annum by FY32, ~78 mtpa including joint ventures) gives investors a specific, trackable growth plan rather than vague expansion ambitions.

The headline ₹16,370 crore Q4 profit figure is overwhelmingly a one-time accounting gain (₹18,051 crore) from the BPSL slump sale — using it to judge 'earnings growth' without adjustment would badly overstate the business's actual improvement, as this dashboard's normalised figure shows.

Leverage remains real even after deleveraging: debt-to-EBITDA of roughly 3.3x on some measures and net debt/EBITDA of 1.81x on others still constrain financial flexibility, and the approved capex plan (₹1,26,000 crore over 4-5 years, plus ₹1,00,000 crore more for JVs/mining) will need continued strong cash generation to fund without leverage creeping back up.

One independent valuation analysis flagged the stock's P/E (near 40x) as 'Expensive' relative to fundamentals, given only 'Average' return ratios (ROE 14%, ROCE 14.5%) — a premium valuation resting heavily on the FY27-FY32 capacity-growth story executing as planned.

India's three business lines all posted record results in the same quarter — Aluminium Upstream EBITDA +13%, Copper EBITDA +48%, Downstream sales volume +18% — broad-based domestic strength, not one segment carrying the group.

PAT before exceptional items reached a record ₹5,796 crore (+10% YoY) even in a quarter Novelis was actively disrupted by fires — real underlying earnings power that the headline reported-PAT decline obscures.

Genuine forward-looking diversification: commissioned a Battery Foil plant and began commercial lithium-ion cell supplies, with an EV battery-enclosure facility at Chakan also scaling up — an early, concrete move into EV/battery materials beyond commodity metal.

The Oswego, US plant fires were a real, large financial hit — ~₹4,171 crore of exceptional charges drove reported consolidated PAT down 50.8% YoY, and Novelis rolled-product shipments (the company's largest single revenue driver) fell 12% YoY as a direct result.
Net debt/EBITDA nearly doubled from 1.06x to 1.83x in a single year — a genuine increase in balance-sheet risk, even if the absolute level remains manageable for now.

Novelis still supplies roughly 60-65% of consolidated revenue and carries its own separate exposures — US tariff policy, plant-specific operational risk, global auto/beverage-can demand cycles — that India's strong domestic results can't fully offset if they persist.

Added a record 9,618 MW of capacity in FY26 — the most in company history — with installed capacity now over 89 GW and another 34+ GW under construction, giving genuine multi-year regulated-return growth already locked into the pipeline.
Coal fleet utilisation (PLF) of 72.04% beats the national average of 63.20% by a wide margin — real, sustained operational excellence, not a one-quarter anomaly.

Balance-sheet discipline is improving alongside the growth: weighted average borrowing cost fell to 5.98% from 6.61%, and outstanding receivable days dropped sharply to 15 from 29 — working-capital and financing efficiency moving the right direction together.

Despite record capacity additions, actual generation fell YoY (432.2 vs 438.7 billion units) on softer demand and grid curtailment — new capacity isn't yet translating into proportionate output or revenue growth.

The headline Q4 profit jump was overwhelmingly driven by a one-off ₹9,062 crore tax credit and regulatory deferral account movements — strip that out and the underlying quarterly operating trend is far more modest than the reported percentage suggests.

NTPC's own renewable capacity addition fell short of its FY26 targets, with projects spilling into FY27 — a real execution gap on the green-transition leg of the growth story, even as the core thermal business performs well.

Works-in-hand of ~₹1.70 lakh crore (over 4x annual revenue) plus a further ₹1.1 lakh crore bidding pipeline give genuinely strong, multi-year revenue visibility — a rare degree of forward clarity for an infrastructure business.
Consultancy revenue grew 56.8% YoY (albeit off a small base) — a real, if modest, diversification beyond the core regulated-tariff transmission business.
One of India's most consistent dividend payers, with a total FY26 payout of ₹9/share and a track record of steady, predictable cash distributions that reflect the underlying regulated-earnings model.
EBITDA margin compressed sharply from 83.98% to 75.21% in Q4 — a real deterioration in a business where margins are usually extremely stable, worth understanding before assuming it's a one-off.

Total borrowings rose from ₹1,30,964 crore to ₹1,48,009 crore in a single year to fund the transmission build-out — leverage is increasing alongside growth, and the company approved a further ₹5,000 crore fundraise at the same results meeting.

Core transmission-segment revenue actually declined 2.7% for the year — the growth story depends on new asset capitalisation (₹37,000 crore guided for FY27) converting into tariff income on schedule, not on the existing asset base.

Record consolidated volumes (44+ million tonnes) and 200 million tonnes of annual capacity — the largest cement producer outside China — with the completed India Cements and Kesoram integrations finishing ahead of schedule.

EBITDA per tonne improved 11% YoY to ₹1,253 even while absorbing a real, quantified West Asia-driven cost headwind (~₹120/tonne) — genuine pricing and cost-efficiency execution, not just a favourable environment.

Net debt/EBITDA held to a disciplined 0.94x while simultaneously funding record capex, two acquisitions, and a further 37 million tonnes of already-committed FY28 capacity — growth and balance-sheet discipline happening together.

The acquired India Cements business is still well below group profitability — its EBITDA/tonne of ₹497 in Q4, while improving fast from ₹333 in Q2, remains under half UltraTech's core ₹1,296/tonne — full synergy realisation is a multi-year story, not already banked.

Management explicitly flagged the West Asia conflict as a live, ongoing cost headwind and absorbed ₹90 crore of one-off packaging costs this quarter — geopolitical volatility is a current, not hypothetical, drag on margins.

The company itself states that Q4/FY26 results 'are not comparable' with the prior year because of the mid-cycle acquisitions — meaning some of the impressive headline volume and revenue growth is inorganic, not pure market-share gain worth extrapolating forward.

Every major segment — cement, paints, chemicals, financial services and VSF — grew and improved profitability in the same quarter, a rare 'everything working at once' result for a genuinely diversified holding company.

Birla Opus (paints) grew revenue 19% sequentially and crossed 10% market share (+370bps) — a real, fast challenge to the paints category's historical incumbent, backed by a specific ₹10,000 crore profitable-revenue target by FY28.

Aditya Birla Capital's lending book grew 32% YoY to ₹2.07 lakh crore, and Grasim is committing further capital (₹2,880 crore) to defend its majority stake as ABCL raises equity — the financial-services arm is becoming a large, fast-growing business in its own right.

Grasim's own standalone results showed a net loss (₹163.54 crore) in Q4, driven by a chemical-plant impairment and new Labour Code cost impacts — the consolidated profit story leans heavily on subsidiaries, and the parent's own core businesses had a genuinely rough quarter.

As a holding company spanning cement, chemicals, textiles, paints and NBFC lending, Grasim bundles businesses with fundamentally different risk profiles into one stock — a regulated-ish industrial cement business sits alongside a credit-risk-bearing financial-services arm, making this a genuinely complex single investment thesis to underwrite.

Birla Opus (paints), while growing fast, is still loss-making as it scales — management's own guidance is that losses 'should reduce in FY27,' meaning the payoff on this multi-year bet is still ahead of investors, not behind them.

Genuinely 'all-round' performance in management's own words — double-digit volume AND value growth alongside meaningful margin expansion, a rare combination after several quarters where the company had to choose between growth and margin.

Raw material deflation (titanium dioxide, crude-linked inputs) directly supported the EBITDA margin recovery to 19.3% from 17.2% — a real cost tailwind that, if sustained, supports FY27 profitability independent of pricing actions.
International operations delivered 'resilient growth with improved profitability despite volatility in select markets' — geographic diversification cushioning any single-market softness in India.

The headline 69% profit jump leans partly on a weak year-ago comparison base (a ₹183 crore exceptional item in Q4 FY25) — the cleaner pre-exceptional profit growth of 33.9% YoY is still strong, but meaningfully less dramatic than the headline number.

Management explicitly named Birla Opus (Grasim's paints challenger, which crossed 10% market share this same quarter) as a genuine FY27 competitive-intensity risk — Asian Paints faces its first well-capitalised direct challenger in years, not just diffuse regional competition.

Management's own outlook flagged the West Asia conflict as contributing to 'near-term uncertainty in demand' — the same geopolitical volatility hitting cement and steel input costs elsewhere in this dashboard also clouds paint demand visibility.

Buyer growth recovered to high-single-digits in Q4 after being nearly flat for three straight quarters — genuine demand recovery, corroborated by like-for-like growth across retail formats of roughly 48% YoY, not just gold-price-driven value inflation.

Consumer (secondary) sales — the cleanest read on real end-customer demand — grew 52% YoY, with both flagship brand Tanishq and youth-focused Mia posting robust growth, evidence of broad-based brand strength rather than a single-format fluke.

The Damas acquisition gives Titan a genuine platform to scale jewellery retail internationally beyond India for the first time at real scale, diversifying a historically India-concentrated growth base.

The headline 80%+ reported revenue growth is significantly inflated by rising gold prices and bullion-related sales that carry much thinner margins than core jewellery retail — reading only the top-line number risks overstating the real growth in Titan's profitable core business.
PAT grew a much more modest 35% YoY and fell 30% sequentially — profitability growth lagged reported revenue growth by a wide margin, consistent with the bullion-distortion point above.
The smart watch segment declined a sharp 53% YoY within Watches & Wearables — a real competitive/category headwind, even as analog watches (Titan, Sonata) grew a healthy 16% on premiumisation trends.

New Business Premium grew a strong 20% YoY for the full year, broad-based across group savings (+55%), annuity (+34%) and par (+123%) — genuine diversification beyond the historically ULIP-heavy product mix (ULIPs were up just 2%).
Embedded Value grew 15% YoY to ₹80,790 crore with a strong 19.7% Return on Embedded Value — real, compounding value creation, not merely premium volume growth.
An unmatched bancassurance distribution moat via SBI's 29,000+ branch network, contributing roughly 60% of APE — a structural advantage no purely private-sector insurer can replicate at this scale.

VNB margin compressed from 30.6% to 28.35% in Q4 specifically, driven by GST-related input tax credit losses — a real, current, sector-wide profitability headwind on new business, even as absolute VNB still grew modestly for the full year.

Q4 PAT declined marginally (~1%) and Q4 APE growth slowed sharply to just 5.5% YoY versus the 13.4% full-year pace — this specific quarter showed real deceleration, not a continuation of earlier momentum.

AUM actually fell 4.8% quarter-on-quarter in Q4 due to equity and debt market pressure — a reminder that a meaningful share of SBI Life's asset base (and future fee income, via ULIPs) is market-linked and directly exposed to market downturns.

Retail protection — the highest-quality, most profitable product category — grew a strong 46% in Q4 (43% for FY26), with its share of the overall mix expanding nearly 200bps YoY, a genuine, deliberate shift toward better-quality, longer-duration business.

Excluding the one-off impact of GST changes and new surrender regulations, VNB margin would have held flat at 25.5% and VNB growth would have tracked APE closely — the underlying business, stripped of regulatory noise, is performing roughly to plan.

AUM grew 12% YoY to ₹3.75 lakh crore alongside a 99.8% claim settlement ratio — steady asset growth paired with genuinely excellent claims-servicing quality, a real trust signal for a business built on long-duration promises.

Headline VNB margin fell to 24.2% for FY26 — a real decline, and even management's own 'excluding GST/surrender impact' framing concedes underlying profitability on new business is under genuine pressure this year, not just a presentation artefact.

APE growth of just 8% YoY for FY26 is meaningfully slower than the ~13-14% pace SBI Life delivered over the same period — HDFC Life's new-business volume momentum currently lags the sector's strongest player on this dashboard.

Q4 net profit grew just 4% YoY off an already modest base, and operating RoEV (15.6%) sits well below SBI Life's 19.7% — the near-term earnings and value-creation story here reads as steady rather than fast-compounding.
FY26 constant-currency revenue growth of 3.9% came in even amid what management itself called 'an uncertain demand environment' — modest but genuine execution against a tough industry-wide backdrop.
Advanced AI revenue crossed $620 million annualized in Q4 — a newly and explicitly disclosed growth line, evidence the AI-services pivot is becoming a real, trackable part of the business.

A highly shareholder-friendly capital allocation policy — 97.6% of FY26 net income returned via dividends (₹60/share for the year), with the board extending a 5-year commitment to return at least 75% of net income to shareholders.

Constant-currency revenue grew just 2.4% YoY in Q4 and fell 3.3% sequentially — the market punished this with a roughly 10% stock decline on results day, reading it as evidence of real, current demand softness in telecom and the end of two SAP programs, not just noise.
FY26 net income was roughly flat YoY despite 11.2% revenue growth — margin compression (partly from the Labour Code cost impact) absorbed essentially all of the topline growth at the bottom line.
FY27 guidance of just 1.0-4.0% constant-currency revenue growth signals management itself expects continued near-term softness, not confident acceleration.
EBIT margin expanded for a tenth consecutive quarter to 13.8% — a genuinely sustained, multi-year turnaround, not a one-quarter blip, explicitly called out by the CFO.

Full-year deal TCV of $3.79 billion (+41.6% YoY) was the highest in five years, while revenue concentration in the top 5 clients actually declined from 15.5% to 14.9% — real diversification alongside real demand momentum, not growing dependency on a few large accounts.
Free cash flow reached 115% of reported PAT for FY26 — genuinely strong cash conversion, evidence the earnings recovery isn't relying on accounting flexibility.

Q4 net profit of ₹1,354 crore missed analyst estimates by roughly 10%, and the constant-currency growth (just 2.4% YoY) driving the 'recovery' narrative depends partly on a rupee tailwind that independent analysts note 'will not be infinite.'

Even after ten quarters of improvement, the 13.8% EBIT margin remains the lowest among the four IT companies on this dashboard — reaching the FY27 target of ~15% requires continued, uninterrupted execution with limited room for error.

The stock's P/E (around 32x) sits at a significant premium to larger, more profitable peers (Infosys ~18-19x, Wipro ~16x, HCLTech ~23x) and 61% above its own 10-year median — the market has already priced in much of the turnaround succeeding, leaving less room for disappointment.

Operating margin held at 17.3% even amid weak revenue growth — genuine, disciplined cost optimisation (a declining employee-cost-to-revenue ratio) that other IT companies with faster growth haven't needed to demonstrate as clearly.

Large deal bookings surged 45.4% YoY for the full year to $7.8 billion, and total bookings grew 14% YoY to $16.4 billion — real forward-order-book momentum that could support a growth recovery if it converts to billing on schedule.

The board approved Wipro's first share buyback in three years (₹15,000 crore at ₹250/share) alongside continued dividends — a sizeable, concrete capital-return signal during a period of weak growth, not just a promise.

Constant-currency, organic growth was genuinely weak this quarter — independent analysis flagged an organic decline of roughly 1.3% QoQ, with FY26 revenue essentially flat-to-down on an organic basis, meaningfully underperforming TCS, Infosys and HCLTech.
Net profit fell 1.9% YoY despite the margin improvement — even disciplined cost management couldn't fully offset weak top-line momentum this quarter.

Five-year EBIT CAGR of just 5.44% trails Wipro's own revenue growth over the same period, per independent analysis — a structural profitability-growth challenge that one strong quarter of margin recovery doesn't resolve.

Genuinely volume-led growth (~18%) rather than price-led is the highest-quality kind of FMCG growth — achieved even as the broader sector deals with urban consumption softness, with market share gains across every product category simultaneously.

EBITDA margin held at a healthy 26.3% even as A&P spending rose over 50% YoY — the volume growth is genuinely absorbing the higher marketing investment with room to spare, not being funded by margin sacrifice.

Cash generation nearly doubled YoY (₹5,047.6 crore vs ₹2,934.5 crore in FY25) and the balance sheet is now clear of short-term debt — real, not just paper, financial strength underpinning the growth story.

The >50% increase in A&P spending to sustain volume momentum raises a genuine sustainability question — if this level of marketing investment is now required to maintain double-digit volume growth, underlying organic demand strength may be less than the headline growth rate implies.

Rising input costs (coffee, cocoa, crude-linked packaging) plus a forecast drier monsoon (a risk to rural demand) create real margin and demand headwinds heading into FY27, with brokerages already flagging possible 3-4% price hikes in Q1 FY27.
The stock trades at a rich ~80x FY26 P/E — a premium valuation that assumes this exceptional, ad-spend-fuelled growth rate is sustainable, leaving little room for a disappointing quarter.

India volume growth of 16% is industry-leading among FMCG peers this quarter, with particularly strong traction in Salt and the Sampann pulses/spices brand — genuine category-level demand strength, not just price-led growth.

EBITDA margin expanded to a multi-quarter high of 14.57% (from 13.47%), with management guiding a further 50-75bps expansion in FY27 — a credible, quantified margin-improvement trajectory, not just an aspiration.
Quick-commerce and e-commerce channels grew over 62% in FY26 — genuine share capture in India's fastest-growing retail channel, not just defending the traditional general-trade base.

Return on Capital Employed of just 9.02% is dramatically weaker than FMCG peers on this dashboard (HUL 31.61%, ITC 44.26%) — reflecting the capital-intensive, still-integrating nature of a business built substantially through acquisitions.

The stock trades at a rich ~80x P/E — the second-highest multiple among the FMCG names covered here — despite delivering by far the weakest capital-efficiency metrics in the group, a genuine valuation-versus-fundamentals tension worth understanding before assuming the multiple is justified.

Five-year sales growth of 12.15%, while ahead of some peers, has come partly through acquisitions rather than pure organic execution — worth separating inorganic contribution from genuine like-for-like brand strength when assessing growth quality.

India business (One-India) grew a strong 15% YoY in Q4, crossing the ₹12,500 crore annual revenue milestone for the first time, with broad-based double-digit growth across respiratory, urology, anti-diabetes and cardiac therapies — the domestic franchise remains genuinely healthy even as the US business struggles.
Africa and emerging markets crossed the $400 million annual revenue milestone with 7-8% YoY growth — real geographic diversification cushioning the North America decline, not just a India-only story.

Management laid out a specific, checkable FY27 recovery roadmap — four respiratory launches and one major peptide launch in the US, targeting a $1 billion US revenue run-rate and 18.5-20% EBITDA margins — a concrete plan with named milestones, not vague reassurance.

North America revenue fell a sharp 26% YoY, driven by a temporary halt in Lanreotide manufacturing at a Greek contract manufacturer (a USFDA observation) and the loss of generic Revlimid exclusivity — two of Cipla's most profitable US products losing earning power at the same time, a real concentration-risk failure, not gradual softening.
EBITDA margin collapsed to 14.6% from 22.8% a year ago — a severe compression, not a modest miss, and full-year PAT fell 26-27% YoY despite record annual revenue.

Cipla's own FY27 EBITDA margin guidance (18.5-20%) explicitly excludes any assumed Lanreotide recovery — management itself isn't counting on a quick fix to the specific product that caused this quarter's collapse.

Highest-ever quarterly revenue (₹6,605 crore) and operating margin (16.7%, up from 13.76% a year ago on one measure) show genuine operating leverage — growth and margin expansion happening together, not a trade-off.

Apollo HealthCo (pharmacy + digital) posted its first full year of profitability in FY26 — EBITDA up from ₹168 crore to ₹488 crore, PAT from ₹47 crore to ₹324 crore — the long-heralded digital/pharmacy optionality is now demonstrably real, not just a future promise.
ARPOB grew 12% YoY even as occupancy also improved — pricing/mix strength and volume growth happening simultaneously, a genuinely healthy combination for a hospital operator.

The core hospital segment's operating margin actually moderated 45bps YoY to 23.8% even as the overall group margin improved — the margin story this quarter is more about the pharmacy/digital arm's turnaround than the core hospital business getting structurally more profitable.

A large capex pipeline (4,400 planned new beds, roughly ₹5,100 crore of remaining spend over five years) means continued heavy capital intensity — management itself flagged around ₹150 crore of expected EBITDA losses from newly commissioned hospitals as they ramp up, a real near-term drag before they mature.

The planned demerger of the pharmacy/digital business by Q4 FY27, while framed as unlocking value, means investors are also betting on a significant corporate restructuring executing cleanly and on schedule — a real execution risk, not yet resolved.

ARPOB of ₹77,900+ is among the highest in the Indian hospital sector, backed by occupancy held above 75% — Max has built a genuinely premium-positioned network rather than competing primarily on volume or scale.

Net debt of just ₹1,908 crore against net debt/EBITDA below 1x, combined with strong free cash flow (₹1,541 crore for FY26) — a financially disciplined balance sheet with real capacity to self-fund expansion without excessive leverage.

The Kalinga Hospital acquisition marks Max's first entry into Eastern India — a genuine new-market growth vector beyond its historical North India concentration, backed by disciplined capital allocation (22% pre-tax ROCE).

Q4 revenue growth of just 10% YoY is meaningfully slower than Apollo's 18% — Max's premium positioning and already-high occupancy (75%+) may leave less headroom for volume-driven growth without new bed capacity coming online.

Historical geographic concentration in North India (Delhi NCR) means Max has been more exposed to regional demand and regulatory dynamics than more geographically diversified peers like Apollo — the Kalinga acquisition is a first step toward diversification, not yet a completed transition.

With occupancy already running above 75%, further growth increasingly depends on capital-intensive bed-capacity expansion (brownfield/greenfield) rather than simply filling more of the existing network — a more expensive growth path than utilisation improvement alone.

Record order book of ₹7.4 lakh crore (+28% YoY) provides roughly 2.6x FY26 revenue in already-secured future work — genuinely exceptional multi-year revenue visibility for a project-execution business.

FY26 recurring PAT (the cleaner, less one-off-distorted profit measure) grew a strong 18% YoY to ₹17,238 crore on record order inflows of ₹4.36 lakh crore (+22% YoY) — real, broad-based execution and demand strength, not accounting noise.

International orders now make up 52% of the total order book — genuine geographic diversification beyond India-only infrastructure spending cycles, with a large ₹17.8 lakh crore addressable prospects pipeline for FY27.

EBITDA margin contracted to 10.4% from 11.0% YoY, and management's own FY27 guidance flags 'flattish' project margins (~7.8%) — even with strong revenue growth, per-project profitability isn't improving, and management isn't promising it will soon.

Heavy Middle East order-book concentration (~40% of total, ~78% of the international book) is a real geopolitical exposure — management itself flagged that supply chain disruptions from the regional conflict will 'dampen' execution in H1 FY27, a concrete, near-term guided headwind.

Reported Q4 PAT actually fell 3% YoY (versus the cleaner recurring PAT's +18% full-year growth) due to a swing in one-off exceptional items — a reminder that L&T's headline quarterly profit can be noisy period to period.

FY26 revenue and profit grew a strong 16% and 14% respectively, with margins holding near management's own 27%+ guidance — genuine execution against a large, growing order book, not just favourable one-off timing.

A debt-free balance sheet with fortress-level cash and deposits (~₹8,572 crore) gives BEL exceptional financial flexibility to invest in R&D and capacity without any leverage risk — rare for a capital-intensive manufacturer.

Order book of ₹73,882 crore provides roughly 2.7x FY26 revenue in already-secured future work, with strong FY26 order inflow (~₹30,000 crore) including a growing export component — genuine multi-year visibility backed by India's structural defence-spending increase.
EBITDA margin contracted to 29.1% from 30.7% YoY — employee costs and other expenses grew faster than revenue, a real cost-discipline question even as the topline growth story stays intact.

Free cash flow conversion is notably thin (roughly 9% per one independent analysis) despite strong reported profit, with a large and growing receivables book — defence PSU customers (primarily government entities) are typically slow payers, a working-capital dynamic that could pressure returns if it doesn't normalise.

Revenue is heavily back-half/Q4-loaded (roughly 37% of full-year revenue in one quarter) — a structural execution-timing concentration that makes any single quarter's results disproportionately important and potentially volatile if year-end order execution slips.

Became the first Indian integrated transport company to handle over 500 million tonnes of cargo in a single financial year, with results exceeding the company's own FY26 guidance on revenue, EBITDA and capex simultaneously.

International ports EBITDA surged 180% YoY with margins reaching a record ~29% (up from 13.7%) — the overseas portfolio (Australia, Colombo) is now genuinely accretive to consolidated profitability, not just a growth-stage cost centre.

Logistics (+55% YoY) and Marine (+134% YoY) are scaling explosively as APSEZ deliberately diversifies beyond pure port cargo handling into an integrated, end-to-end supply chain platform — real business-model evolution.

Net debt/EBITDA of 1.9x reflects continued heavy capex to fund the ports-to-logistics transformation and international expansion — a real, ongoing leverage commitment tied to execution of an ambitious FY31 target (1 billion tonnes of cargo by December 2030).

Q4 PAT growth of 9.4% lagged well behind revenue growth of 26% — results were specifically affected by depreciation on newly commissioned assets, meaning near-term reported profit doesn't yet fully reflect the earnings power of the capital being deployed.

As part of the broader Adani Group, APSEZ carries governance and disclosure scrutiny that peer infrastructure companies without the same corporate-group history don't face to the same degree — a real, ongoing consideration for investors independent of operational performance.

80% of consolidated EBITDA now comes from mature, long-term, contracted businesses (core infrastructure and mining services) — a genuine, disclosed shift toward earnings stability as the incubation portfolio matures, not just an aspiration.

Major infrastructure milestones landed in FY26: the Ganga Expressway (India's largest greenfield expressway project) completed in under 3.5 years, plus continued build-out of Navi Mumbai and Guwahati airports — tangible execution of a genuinely large infrastructure pipeline.

Solar module manufacturing sales surged 95% YoY and data center capacity keeps scaling (a new 358MW hyperscale order in Hyderabad) — the newer 'incubating' businesses are showing real commercial traction, not just capex spend.

Adani Enterprises swung to a net loss of ₹221 crore in Q4 (from a ₹3,845 crore profit a year earlier), driven by higher depreciation on newly commissioned assets (Navi Mumbai Airport, the Kutch copper plant) — a real, current earnings hit from the capex-heavy incubation model.

FY26's headline 31% PAT growth is overwhelmingly attributable to a one-off ₹9,215 crore exceptional gain from selling stakes in Adani Wilmar and cement units — full-year EBITDA (the cleaner operating measure) was essentially flat YoY, a much less impressive underlying trend than the reported PAT growth suggests.

As part of the broader Adani Group, AEL carries the same governance and disclosure scrutiny as its listed affiliates — a real, ongoing consideration for investors independent of the company's own operational execution.

Even in what management called 'an exceptionally challenging operating environment,' IndiGo delivered ₹7,503 crore of profit excluding forex and exceptional items for FY26, while growing capacity 9.5% and maintaining a strong balance sheet with over ₹5.1 lakh crore of total cash — real resilience, not just survival.
Fuel costs (fuel CASK) fell 11.5% YoY, a genuine tailwind from lower crude prices, partially offsetting non-fuel cost inflation.

A deliberate internationalisation and premiumisation push (business class introduction, loyalty programme, long-haul wide-body and XLR aircraft induction, new routes like Delhi/Mumbai-Athens) targets higher-yield segments beyond the traditional domestic low-cost model — genuine strategic evolution, not just capacity expansion.

IndiGo swung to a full-year net loss of ₹2,394 crore (from a substantial profit the prior year) — even excluding forex and exceptional items, the 'clean' profit figure fell 15.4% YoY, meaning underlying profitability genuinely deteriorated, not just currency-swing optics.

Total costs grew 17.2% YoY for the full year against just 6.4% total income growth — a serious, structural cost-growth-outpacing-revenue problem, with non-fuel unit costs (CASK ex-fuel) rising 3.8% even as fuel costs fell, meaning non-fuel costs are the real pressure point.

Sharp rupee depreciation and new labour-code-related costs were both explicitly named by management as offsetting operational gains — real, current macro and regulatory headwinds outside the company's direct control, on top of Middle East conflict-related capacity disruptions.

The lending arm (Bajaj Finance) delivered strongly — AUM up 22% YoY to ₹5.09 lakh crore with Q4 net total income up 21% — genuinely carrying group growth while other segments disappointed, a proven, market-leading consumer-lending franchise.

Excluding one-off mark-to-market portfolio losses from market volatility, Q4 revenue growth would have been 14% (versus the 6% reported) — the underlying operating momentum is materially stronger than the headline number suggests.

Full ownership of the insurance businesses (Bajaj bought out Allianz's 24-year JV stake for ~₹21,390 crore) gives the group complete strategic control and flexibility over product launches and decisions for the first time — a genuine structural change, not just a shareholding reshuffle.

The general insurance arm genuinely disappointed — Q4 Gross Written Premium was flat (~9% below analyst targets) and Q4 profit grew just 2% (26% below targets) — a real, current weak spot in one of the group's three core businesses, at a time the insurance industry is expected to grow ~10% CAGR.

Full-year PAT growth of 10% is well below the group's historical 20%+ pace, and independent analysts flag margin pressure across lending and insurance from elevated funding costs and regulatory scrutiny on unsecured lending — a genuinely tougher operating environment, not a one-quarter blip.

Now that Bajaj fully owns the insurance businesses, it carries 100% of their execution risk directly — the same insurance weakness that showed up this quarter now flows entirely to Bajaj Finserv rather than being shared with a JV partner, and emerging businesses (health, digital, AMC) remain loss-making (₹539 crore drag in FY26).

Revenue nearly doubled YoY (Q4 +106.5%, FY26 +78%) with lending AUM up 156% — genuinely explosive scaling across lending, payments, asset management and insurance broking simultaneously, all driven by organic growth rather than acquisitions.

Core operating income now drives 54% of net total income (up from 20% a year ago) — a real, structural shift away from passive treasury/dividend income toward a genuine operating financial-services business, exactly the maturation the thesis requires.

The Jio BlackRock AMC reached ₹15,200+ crore AUM with over 40% of inflows from beyond India's top 30 cities — evidence the Reliance-scale distribution advantage (450M+ Jio subscribers, 19,000+ pin codes) is translating into genuine financial-product reach, not just a theoretical edge.

Consolidated PAT fell 14% YoY as total expenses surged 327% — this is a company deliberately sacrificing near-term profit for growth, which means investors are underwriting 3-5 years of continued execution before profitability catches up to the revenue scaling, with no guarantee it does.

At a ~₹1.55 lakh crore market cap against current profitability, the valuation prices in substantial future success that hasn't yet been proven — the buildout is real, but so is the execution risk in a fiercely competitive digital-lending and payments market.

Treasury volatility (from late-March market/geopolitical stress) hit both treasury income and JioBlackRock's AUM this quarter — a reminder that a meaningful part of the current asset base is market-linked and exposed to downturns while the core operating businesses are still too young to fully offset it.

Blinkit turned Adjusted-EBITDA positive for the first time (₹37 crore) while NOV surged 95.4% YoY — the single biggest milestone this quarter, proving quick commerce can translate explosive scale into actual operating profit, not just growth.

109 million Indians transacted over $10 billion through the platform in FY26, with Blinkit's NOV compounding at 104% CAGR from FY23-26 — genuine, category-defining scale, and management targets $1 billion in Adjusted EBITDA by FY29 (roughly 8x in five years from an already-profitable base).

Quick commerce is still concentrated in the top 15-20 cities and a narrow product range (Delhi NCR offers ~80,000 SKUs versus ~20,000 in newer markets) — geographic expansion, assortment deepening and demand densification are all still early innings, each compounding on the others.

Full-year FY26 PAT actually declined to ₹366 crore (from ₹527 crore) despite the explosive revenue growth — profitability is being deliberately sacrificed for expansion, so investors are underwriting continued execution rather than banking current earnings.

The headline 196% reported revenue growth is mechanically inflated by Blinkit's marketplace-to-inventory accounting shift — the honest like-for-like figure is 64%, and reading the headline number badly overstates the real underlying growth rate.

Competitive intensity in quick commerce is genuinely fierce and repeatedly flagged by analysts — Zepto and other well-funded rivals are building speed and density, meaning Blinkit's lead and margins must be defended continuously against deep-pocketed competition, not assumed as durable.

Explosive, disciplined store expansion — 212 new Zudio stores in FY26 (~1.2 per day in Q4), with ~80% of new stores in Tier 2/3 cities targeting underpenetrated, faster-growing markets — a genuine, repeatable growth engine, not a one-off.

EBITDA margin expanded 242bps YoY in Q4 even while expanding aggressively, driven by better product mix, lower discounting, and falling employee/rent cost per square foot (aided by RFID supply-chain integration) — real operating leverage kicking in as scale builds.

Emerging categories (beauty, innerwear, footwear) now contribute over 21% of revenue and Westside online grew 25% — genuine diversification beyond core apparel, plus a maiden 1:2 bonus issue signalling management's confidence in the medium-term outlook.

Like-for-like (same-store) growth stayed in low single digits and revenue per square foot actually declined ~6% YoY — the impressive 19% headline revenue growth is almost entirely new-store-driven, meaning the existing store base is barely growing, a genuine quality-of-growth concern.

The stock trades at a very rich ~95x P/E (per one analysis) with a PEG around 8x — a valuation leaving minimal room for execution missteps, especially as Q4 PAT fell ~22% sequentially and FII holdings declined from ~19.7% to ~15.6% over the year.

Management explicitly flagged cautious discretionary consumer sentiment (geopolitical uncertainty, inflation, cost-of-living concerns) softening as Q4 progressed — a real demand-side headwind for a value-fashion retailer whose growth depends on continued consumer willingness to spend.
Added a record 5.1 GW of greenfield capacity in FY26 — the highest of any company outside China — taking total operating capacity to 19.3 GW, up 35% YoY.
Zero promoter share pledging and an inaugural JCR BBB+ credit rating, aligned with India's own sovereign rating, signal growing financial credibility despite the scale of the build-out.

28 GW of capacity is already signed against the 50 GW-by-2030 target, with over 90% of the portfolio locked into long-term PPAs — unusually strong multi-year revenue visibility for a growth-stage utility.
Net debt/EBITDA near 11x and net debt/equity around 4.2x are extreme even by capital-intensive utility standards — real refinancing risk if rates or funding conditions tighten.
Grid curtailment cost the company an estimated ₹1,300–1,500 crore of EBITDA in FY26 alone — transmission build-out (much of it via a sister Adani company) hasn't kept pace with generation capacity.
FII holding has been drifting down, and at a P/E north of 100x the stock prices in years of flawless execution, leaving little room for disappointment.
FY26 revenue grew 54% to ₹16,679 crore with EBITDA margin expanding to 18.1% — scale is now translating into both growth and profitability, not just one or the other.

A ₹2,384 crore NET CASH position as of March 2026 is a remarkable turnaround for a company that went through Corporate Debt Restructuring in 2015 — the balance-sheet risk that once defined this stock is largely gone.
Order book of ~5.9 GW, with 66% from PSU and commercial/industrial customers, gives multi-quarter revenue visibility in a domestic wind market targeting 100 GW by 2030.
At a P/E near 25x and P/B over 8x, the market is already pricing in continued high growth — any order-book slowdown would hit the stock hard.
FY26 net profit included a large deferred-tax-asset benefit; core operating profit growth, while strong, is smaller than the headline PAT growth suggests on its own.
More than 90% of revenue is domestic-India — a slowdown in Indian wind tenders or a policy shift would hit Suzlon far harder than globally diversified equipment peers.
FY26 revenue more than doubled effectively — up 83.7% to ₹26,537 crore — while PAT grew even faster at 101.4% to ₹3,884 crore, meaning profitability scaled faster than the topline itself.
Order book of ~₹53,000 crore against a pipeline exceeding 100 GW gives multi-year revenue visibility, and Waaree is the largest non-Chinese solar module manufacturer globally at ~26 GW capacity.

Backward integration into ingots, wafers, PV glass, batteries and a US manufacturing base (Brookings, South Dakota) reduces reliance on any single input or market, and the company pays a real dividend (₹4/share FY26) unlike most high-growth sector peers.

Free cash flow was deeply negative in FY26 — inventory and receivables grew faster than profits, meaning P&L strength hasn't yet been matched by cash generation; this working-capital cycle is the key swing factor for FY27.
Q4 FY26 margins showed signs of moderation even as revenue doubled, and depreciation nearly tripled (₹402 crore to ₹990 crore) as the aggressive capacity build-out weighs on the balance sheet.

A significant share of revenue comes from US exports, and the company was recently examined by US Customs over anti-circumvention allegations (cleared, per the company) — a reminder that trade-policy risk sits directly on top of the growth story.

Backed by NTPC Ltd. (a Maharatna CPSE) which owns ~89% of the company, giving access to government-linked project pipelines, cheaper financing, and credibility to win large PSU-anchored tenders private developers can't easily match.
FY26 revenue grew to ₹2,858 crore and Q4 revenue hit a record ₹913 crore (+46.7% YoY), with the company already the largest by market capitalisation in the power sector.

Part of NTPC group's 60 GW renewable-by-2032 target, with a 30 GW project pipeline and new joint ventures (including with ONGC and a data-centre-focused tie-up with CtrlS) extending its addressable market.
Return on equity of just 3.24% is weak by any standard, and at a P/E north of 150x, the stock is priced for a growth story that current capital efficiency doesn't yet support.
Debt-funded expansion is accelerating faster than earnings: non-current borrowings jumped from ₹17,301 crore to ₹28,637 crore in a single year, pushing the debt-to-equity ratio from 0.97 to 1.54.
