Interest coverage of just 1.49x leaves little cushion — rising interest costs (up 45.6% YoY in Q4 alone) are already eating into the operating profit that revenue growth is generating.

FY26 net profit grew 16% to ₹4,256 crore, sales bookings met the ₹20,000 crore guidance at ₹20,143 crore, and the development business now carries zero gross debt with a net cash surplus — a striking balance-sheet position for a real-estate developer.

The annuity business (leased offices, malls, hospitality) gives DLF a second, steadier income stream on top of the lumpy, cyclical project-sales business, insulating it somewhat from housing-market swings.
Super-luxury launches like Dahlias generated ₹3,967 crore of bookings in a single quarter, showing DLF can command premium pricing in India's high-end residential upcycle.
Q4 alone told a weaker story: EBITDA fell 42.3% YoY and margin contracted to 28.2% from 31.3%, missing analyst estimates on revenue, EBITDA and margin all at once.
Full-year sales bookings actually declined about 5% YoY even though they met guidance — growth has plateaued rather than accelerated.
Return on equity of just 9.37% is modest for a company trading near 40x earnings — the market is paying a growth premium that recent bookings data hasn't clearly justified.

FY26 net profit grew 19.25% to ₹57,419 crore on Total Premium Income of ₹5,35,984 crore, and remains India's overwhelming market leader with 56.66% share of new business premium — more than the rest of the industry's private insurers combined.

Value of New Business (VNB) grew 41.63% to ₹14,179 crore with margin above 21%, and the Non-Par product share rose sharply to 35.11% from 27.69% — the mix is shifting toward higher-margin products, not just growing in volume.

Trading at a trailing P/E near 9.5x against an ROE near 40%+, LIC is priced far cheaper than most large-cap financials despite a solvency ratio that improved to 2.35 from 2.11, comfortably above the regulatory minimum.

Persistency ratios — how many policyholders keep paying premiums over time — slipped slightly (13th month 67.77% vs 68.62%, 61st month 54.13% vs 58.54%), a real quality signal worth watching even amid strong headline growth.
Market share in new business premium edged down from 57.05% to 56.66% as private insurers continue to chip away at LIC's historic dominance, particularly in higher-margin non-par and ULIP products.

The Government of India holds 96.5% of the company and media reports point to a possible further stake sale — any large secondary share sale could pressure the stock regardless of underlying business performance.
FY26 revenue grew 11.8% to ₹14,601 crore with EBITDA margin expanding to 24.2%, and Q4 alone saw PAT jump 36.6% — profit growing meaningfully faster than sales as input costs eased.

Iconic, hard-to-displace consumer brands (Fevicol, Dr. Fixit, M-Seal, Fevikwik) give Pidilite pricing power that generic adhesive and chemical makers lack, evident in a payout ratio of 70.2% — the business generates more cash than it needs to reinvest.
Double-digit volume growth across both the Consumer & Bazaar and Business-to-Business segments shows demand strength isn't concentrated in one channel or product line.
Working capital days jumped from 51.2 to 116 — more than doubling — a real deterioration in how quickly the company converts sales into cash, even as the income statement looks strong.

Margins benefited significantly from lower vinyl acetate monomer and crude-linked input costs this year; a reversal in those raw material prices would directly compress the margin expansion investors are currently rewarding.

At roughly 62–66x trailing earnings, the stock prices in continued flawless execution, leaving little room for a miss even though B2B export volumes actually declined amid Middle East-related disruption.

FY26 consolidated PAT nearly doubled, up 93.78% YoY to ₹25,843 crore, as gross refining margins recovered sharply to $11.74/barrel from a much weaker prior year, alongside record refinery throughput of 41.15 MMT at 116% utilisation.
Domestic fuel demand kept growing across the board — petrol +5.7%, diesel +1%, ATF +11.4%, and CNG sales surged 62.1% — showing volume growth well beyond just a margin-driven profit story.
At a trailing P/E near 5x with an ROE near 28%, BPCL trades at a steep discount to the broader market despite genuinely strong FY26 execution and continued government backing as a Maharatna PSU.

Refining margins are a volatile, largely externally-determined number — the same $11.74/barrel GRM that nearly doubled profit this year can just as easily collapse in a weaker global refining environment, as FY25's much lower margins already showed.

The Exploration & Production segment (via subsidiary BPRL, including the Mozambique LNG project) remains loss-making, though the loss narrowed as a force-majeure situation resolved — a reminder that not every part of the business is profitable yet.

As a PSU oil marketing company, BPCL's retail fuel pricing and subsidy exposure are ultimately subject to government policy, not pure market economics — a structural risk private-sector peers don't carry.

Q4 CY25 order inflow surged 52% YoY to ₹4,096 crore — the strongest Q4 order intake in five years — with a large data centre order now representing 10-11% of the total order book, tapping directly into India's AI infrastructure buildout.

CY25 revenue hit a record ₹13,203 crore (+8% YoY), with export revenue growing across all major divisions and strategic focus on 23 high-growth segments including renewables, electronics, and grid modernisation.
Backed by decades of ABB Group's global technology and an integrated ecosystem, the company is positioned to benefit from an anticipated resurgence in India's private capex cycle in 2026.

Q4 CY25 net profit fell 18.08% YoY to ₹432.85 crore even as revenue grew, with EBITDA margin contracting roughly 230-400 basis points on higher imported raw material costs tied to new Quality Control Order (QCO) localisation rules.

Material costs rose to 61% of revenue from 58% a year earlier as the company was forced to import more components during a QCO compliance transition period extending into September 2026 — a self-inflicted near-term cost headwind from a policy meant to help domestic manufacturers.

At a P/E near 53-57x, the stock prices in a clean recovery story, but intensifying Chinese competition in the Electrification and Motion segments and ABB Group's global divestment of its Robotics business add real uncertainty.

Q2 FY26 revenue reached a record ₹5,171 crore on strong order execution across infrastructure and industrial segments, with order intake climbing 18% on a comparable basis and record levels across most divisions.

The German parent dramatically consolidated its promoter stake to 75% (from a fragmented ~6.67% base) during the FY26 Energy business demerger, signalling long-term strategic commitment to the Indian subsidiary even as the corporate structure was reorganised.

Siemens India sits at the intersection of India's biggest capex themes — infrastructure, energy transition, digitalisation and urbanisation — with key clients including Power Grid, Indian Railways, data centre operators and state DISCOMs.

Operating margins compressed sharply from 15.34% to 11.93% over six quarters, and Q2 FY26 net profit fell 41.63% YoY even as revenue grew — a genuine disconnect between topline momentum and bottom-line delivery.
Working capital days nearly doubled from 41.9 to 67.6, signalling slower cash conversion despite reported revenue strength.

The FY26 demerger of the Energy business into separately-listed Siemens Energy India makes year-over-year comparisons genuinely difficult — investors are effectively underwriting a smaller, restructured company at a premium valuation built on pre-demerger growth assumptions.

Order book swelled to ₹2,54,538 crore — nearly 8 times FY26 revenue — after ₹97,028 crore of fresh orders including 97 LCA Mark-1A aircraft, giving HAL exceptional multi-year revenue visibility rare in any industry.
FY26 EBITDA grew 11% to ₹13,472 crore while maintaining a healthy 30% margin, and return on equity of 24.71% ranks among the best in the defence sector — trailing only Mazagon Dock.

Trading at a P/E of roughly 32-33x against a defence sector average near 55x, HAL is priced at a meaningful discount to peers despite comparable or superior fundamentals — a genuine relative-value case.

LCA Mark-1A deliveries have faced repeated delays, now targeted for August-September 2026, driven partly by GE Aerospace engine supply shortfalls outside HAL's control — a reminder that even strong order books depend on external supply chains.

Revenue growth of 7% YoY is modest for a company trading at this valuation, and operating margin actually contracted in Q4 FY26 (36.28% from 38.65%) even as headline profit surged on a large jump in other income.

HAL's revenue mix is expected to shift from roughly 30-70 to 50-50 between manufacturing and other work in FY27, a structural change management believes it can navigate — but which adds execution uncertainty to the growth story.
FY26 revenue grew 13.8% to ₹13,006 crore with essentially zero long-term debt and ₹13,097 crore in combined cash and fixed deposits — a fortress balance sheet rare among industrial companies.

Contract Negotiation Committee talks for the P-75(I) submarine program (~₹99,000 crore for six stealth AIP submarines with Germany's TKMS) have been completed and now await government approval — a deal that alone could roughly quadruple the current order book.

The company is actively diversifying beyond pure defence dependency, signing a commercial contract with the Shipping Corporation of India for a methanol dual-fuel vessel — its re-entry into commercial shipbuilding.
The order book has fallen sharply to ₹23,758 crore as of December 2025, less than half the ₹49,700 crore level at the end of FY21 — the P-75(I) deal is a hoped-for catalyst, not yet a booked order.

Q4 FY26 EBITDA margin collapsed to 13.74% from 35.1% in the prior quarter, as the company took provisions on two contracts where management now expects losses — a reminder that shipbuilding margins can swing sharply on project-specific risk.
Receivables spiked 144% year-over-year and contingent liabilities stand at ₹37,852 crore, both worth watching even though the core balance sheet remains fundamentally strong.

Renewables PAT grew 59% to ₹1,994 crore and Solar Manufacturing (TP Solar) PAT grew 103% to ₹857 crore in FY26, with industry-leading module/cell yields above 95% — Tata Power is monetising the energy transition across generation, manufacturing and rooftop simultaneously.

T&D business PAT rose 49% to ₹2,978 crore and Odisha DISCOMs PAT rose 84% to ₹809 crore, showing the distribution and discom-turnaround businesses are genuinely improving, not just the flashier renewables story.

Rooftop Solar (Solaroof) has ranked #1 in India for 11 consecutive years, with FY26 PAT up 150% to ₹499 crore — a high-margin, fast-growing consumer-facing business layered on top of the core utility.

Q4 FY26 revenue fell 12.84% YoY and operating margin dropped to a seven-quarter low of 17.44%, driven partly by the Mundra plant losing Section 11 cost-recovery benefits — a reminder that regulatory support, not just execution, drives parts of this business.
Debt-to-EBITDA of 5.98x and interest coverage of just 1.43x are genuine leverage concerns for a company still funding an 11.6 GW renewable build-out.
Return on equity of just 10.2% and ROCE of 8.0% are weak for a stock trading near 35x earnings — the growth story hasn't yet translated into best-in-class capital returns.

Q4 FY26 net sales reached ₹7,443 crore, the highest quarterly figure on record, with a 5-year sales CAGR of 20.62% and EBIT CAGR of 20.08% demonstrating consistent, structural growth in transmission demand.
Promoter stake increased to 72.73%, signalling Adani family alignment with minority shareholders even as some foreign institutional investors have been selling.

As India's largest private transmission company, AESL is directly positioned to capture the multi-decade build-out needed to connect new renewable capacity (including sister company Adani Green's projects) to the grid — exemplified by its 6,000 MW Integrated Green Energy Corridor project.

Operating margin compressed sharply to 28.82% in Q4 FY26 from 34.71% the prior quarter and 35.31% a year earlier — a 649 basis point year-over-year decline that raises real questions about cost discipline even as revenue grows.
At roughly 73x trailing earnings — nearly three times the sector average — the stock prices in years of flawless execution, and return on equity of just 10.5% doesn't yet justify that premium.
Low interest coverage and continued FII selling, even alongside promoter buying, suggest institutional investors remain divided on whether current valuations reflect the underlying business quality.

Q4 FY26 adjusted revenue grew 38% YoY and EBITDA grew 78% YoY, driven by robust capacity additions and a 48% YoY increase in power sales — genuine operational momentum, not just a base-effect illusion.
Net Debt/EBITDA improved to 6x in FY26 from 7.8x in FY25, with EBITDA per MW and gross block per MW both improving — real evidence that past capacity investments are starting to pay off.

The company is confident of reaching its 30 GW by 2030 target, with 13.4 GW already operational, 14 GW under construction and a further 4.6 GW in the pipeline — exceptional forward capacity visibility.

Adjusted PAT actually fell 36% YoY in Q4 FY26 despite EBITDA growing 78% — rising depreciation and interest costs from the capacity build-out are eating deeply into the bottom line even as operations scale.
The stock fell 8% on results day despite headline-strong numbers, as brokerages flagged expensive valuation (12.5x EBITDA vs 11.3x historical median) following a sharp prior rally.
Promoter holding declined 2.74% over the last quarter and return on equity remains weak at 7.62% — capital efficiency hasn't caught up with the scale of investment yet.
Q4 FY26 PAT grew 64% YoY to ₹4,271 crore on 10% revenue growth and 27% EBITDA growth, with expansion tie-ups now reaching 13.3 GW including a new 1.6 GW long-term PPA awarded by Maharashtra's DISCOM.

As India's largest private thermal power producer with 17.5 GW of operating capacity and a target of 30.6 GW by 2030, Adani Power is positioned to capture a large share of India's continuing baseload power demand even as renewables grow.
All-India power demand growth resumed in Q4 FY26 (+1.6% YoY) after a soft winter, with warmer weather driving renewed offtake — a near-term operational tailwind.

Full-year FY26 EBITDA was essentially flat versus FY25 (₹21,285 crore vs ₹21,575 crore) despite higher operating capacity, as lower merchant power realisations (average IEX price down 13.7% YoY) offset volume gains — growth in assets hasn't yet translated into growth in core earnings.

Adani Power pays no dividend on its equity shares despite consistent profitability, and screening tools flag potential capitalisation of interest costs — a detail worth understanding since it can make reported profits look stronger than underlying cash generation.

Rising renewable power penetration is structurally pressuring merchant thermal tariffs, creating a slow-moving headwind for the non-contracted portion of Adani Power's capacity even as its own PPA-backed volumes stay resilient.

Record Q4 FY26 domestic sales (wholesale volume +8.7% YoY to 208,275 units), highest-ever quarterly and full-year CNG/rural penetration, and 16.4% export growth despite geopolitical headwinds show broad-based demand strength, not just one segment.

Celebrating 30 years in India, the company commenced its Pune plant in FY26 (first product: New Venue, 5-star Bharat NCAP rated), expanding capacity toward 1.14 million units by 2030 — real physical capacity backing the growth guidance.
Zero promoter encumbrance and a stable 82.5% holding from Hyundai Motor Company signal long-term parent commitment, while FY27 guidance targets 8-10% volume growth and margin recovery to 11-14%.

Q4 FY26 operating margin contracted sharply to 10.66% from 14.45% a year earlier — a 379 basis point erosion — driven by vendor compensation, labour code costs, commodity inflation and an unfavourable SUV/export mix.
Net profit fell 22.2% YoY in Q4 FY26 to ₹1,256 crore even as revenue hit a record high, and FY26 full-year PAT was below FY25's — growth in volume hasn't yet translated into growth in profit.

Persistent FII selling (down from 7.35% to 5.42% since September 2025) suggests foreign institutional investors remain cautious on near-term earnings trajectory despite management's optimistic FY27 guidance.

Q4 FY26 net sales hit an all-time high of ₹15,053 crore, up 30.42% YoY, with FY26 full-year net sales of ₹56,070 crore — broad-based demand strength across the two-wheeler and three-wheeler portfolio.

Rock-solid 50.27% promoter holding (TVS Holdings, formerly Sundaram Clayton) with zero pledging reflects strong long-term family commitment, and ten-year stock returns exceeding 1,000% demonstrate a genuine track record of value creation.
Rising EV sales and export expansion give TVS multiple growth vectors beyond the core domestic ICE two-wheeler market, which remains competitive but stable.
Q4 FY26 operating margin contracted to 14.53% from 16.63% a year earlier, and net profit fell 8.29% sequentially despite revenue growth — the classic sign of rising costs outpacing volume gains.
Debt-to-equity of 3.24x and debt-to-EBITDA of 4.17x represent real leverage, and screening tools flag possible capitalisation of interest costs — worth independent scrutiny of reported profitability.
FII holding declined for the first time in four quarters (down 52 basis points to 22.57%), suggesting some foreign investors are booking profits or growing cautious after a strong run.

Q4 FY26 revenue grew 16% YoY to ₹4,911 crore with EBITDA growing 16.1%, and the company declared a substantial ₹512/share dividend — consistent, well-funded shareholder returns from a genuinely diversified technology business.

Operating across four distinct sectors — Mobility, Industrial Technology, Consumer Goods, and Energy and Building Technology — Bosch India isn't purely an auto-cycle bet, giving it more resilience than a pure-play auto component maker.
Backed by Robert Bosch GmbH's global engineering and R&D, with nearly 18 manufacturing units across India, Bosch combines deep local manufacturing scale with world-class parent technology.

At a P/E near 40-43x, the stock is priced at a significant premium, and ongoing corporate restructuring within the broader Bosch India group (including a planned Offer For Sale in Bosch Home Comfort India) adds complexity for investors trying to value the standalone entity.

As a subsidiary of a German multinational, Bosch India's strategic direction — including which businesses stay, get transferred, or get restructured — is ultimately decided at the global parent level, not by Indian shareholders.

Heavy reliance on the automotive sector for its Mobility segment means Bosch India remains meaningfully exposed to the same auto-cycle risks facing its OEM customers like Hyundai and TVS Motor elsewhere in this dataset.

Q4 FY26 consolidated PAT jumped 42.5% YoY to ₹1,497 crore, recovering strongly from a weak Q4 FY25, with an automotive booked business exceeding USD 83.9 billion providing exceptional multi-year revenue visibility.

Operating across four divisions (Modules & Polymer Products, Vision Systems, Wiring Harnesses, and Emerging Businesses including aerospace and consumer electronics) spanning 30+ countries gives genuine diversification against any single customer, geography or auto segment.
Interest costs have been declining steadily as the company deleverages, with interest coverage improving to nearly 9x — a real, ongoing balance-sheet improvement story.
Promoter holding dropped sharply from 58.13% to 48.60% in mid-2025 due to corporate restructuring — a significant ownership change investors should understand rather than overlook.

Return on equity of just 11.6% is weak for a company of this scale, and the wide gap between 5-year sales CAGR and EBIT CAGR points to a persistent struggle to convert growth into proportionate profit.

An aggressive, ongoing string of global acquisitions (Nexans autoelectric, Elektrokontakt, Yutaka Giken, Shinnichi, Vacuform, among others) creates real integration risk even as it extends the company's global footprint.

Consolidated Q4 FY26 net profit surged 49.23% YoY to ₹2,575 crore, reflecting the growing value of Bajaj Holdings' strategic stakes in Bajaj Auto, Bajaj Finserv and Bajaj Finance — the underlying businesses are compounding even when the holding company's own standalone numbers look erratic.

Trading at a P/E of just 16.09x and P/BV of 1.74x, Bajaj Holdings is priced at a meaningful discount to most holding company peers despite a five-year sales CAGR of 22.58% and EBIT CAGR of 27.22% — a classic holding-company-discount opportunity.
Rock-solid 51.46% promoter holding, led by the Bajaj family through Jamnalal Sons Private Limited and various family trusts, has remained completely stable across five straight quarters.

Standalone revenue collapsed 58.32% YoY (and 78.94% sequentially) to just ₹60.54 crore — a reminder that Bajaj Holdings' own operating business is tiny and volatile; nearly all real value sits in its investment stakes, not anything it does directly.

The structural 'holding company discount' is a double-edged sword: investors can simply buy Bajaj Auto, Bajaj Finserv or Bajaj Finance directly instead of paying through this intermediary layer, capping how much re-rating Bajaj Holdings itself can realistically see.

Foreign institutional investors have been steadily reducing their stake (down 94 basis points over four quarters), possibly preferring direct exposure to the operating Bajaj companies over the holding structure.
Q4 FY26 net profit grew 14.06% YoY to ₹669.19 crore with interest income up 15.69%, showing consistent double-digit growth momentum in both the top and bottom line of the loan book.

As a subsidiary of Bajaj Finance (holding an 86.7% stake), Bajaj Housing Finance benefits from the parent's underwriting expertise, funding access and brand trust — real inherited advantages in a competitive mortgage market.

India's RBI rate cut cycle that commenced in 2026 reduces Bajaj Housing Finance's own borrowing costs while also stimulating end-customer demand for home loans — a tailwind on both sides of the balance sheet.
PAT margin compressed slightly to 23.15% from 23.48% a year earlier, and at a P/E near 28x, the stock is priced for continued strong growth that any slowdown would punish quickly.

The company is investing in significant capacity expansion — including a new Greenfield Transformer factory — directly positioning it to capture India's accelerating power infrastructure and grid modernisation investment cycle.

As a newly demerged entity (separated from Siemens Ltd in March 2025), Siemens Energy India has limited independent trading history, making multi-year historical comparisons and valuation benchmarking genuinely difficult.
At a P/B ratio of 26.3x, the stock is priced at a significant premium, leaving little room for execution missteps as the company builds out its independent operating track record.

New Power Transmission orders showed a sequential decline in the first half of the fiscal year compared to the prior year, raising questions about whether order intake momentum can be sustained at the pace embedded in current valuations.
FY26 pre-sales reached ₹20,500 crore, a 28% CAGR since FY21's COVID-low base of ₹6,000 crore, with PAT growing more than six-fold over the same period to ₹3,431 crore at a 20% margin.

Management has set an explicit medium-term target of 20% PAT CAGR, aiming to grow annual profit from ₹3,400 crore (FY26) to more than ₹8,500 crore by FY31 — a clearly communicated, ambitious growth roadmap.

The company is actively expanding beyond its core Mumbai/Pune/Bengaluru markets into Delhi NCR, with ~₹19,000 crore of new project launches planned across its existing core cities, diversifying its geographic growth base.

Return on equity of just 13.8% is modest for a company reporting 20% PAT margins, suggesting a large capital base that isn't yet generating proportionate shareholder returns — worth watching as the company scales.

A former director of the company (prior to its rename from Macrotech Developers) was reported arrested by Mumbai crime branch on matters unrelated to routine business operations — while not a company-wide financial concern, it is a governance matter worth being aware of.

Real estate developer earnings are inherently tied to property market cycles and project completion timing; sustaining a 20% PAT CAGR through FY31 requires consistent execution across multiple new geographic markets simultaneously.
